one_liner: Walk into a library, unban the banned book.
search_doc:
Walk into a library, unban the banned book.
Mechanisms: geofencing-as-activism, library-as-loophole, rights-circumvention, real-time-database
Idea: The Banned Book Club is designed for today and for the future. Whenever a new book is banned, it can be added to our dynamic database, making it available instantly in any library that censors it, even nationwide. As the campaign grows, access is expanding to all of America, regardless of location.
Insight: Using PEN America's Index of Book Bans, the Digital Public Library of America made the most banned books in the U.S. then geofenced the exact footprint of every library with banned books, so that any person who walks into those libraries gains instant access to the banned book.

full_record.file_path: 2024/DIGITAL CRAFT/BRONZE/Banned Book Club - Digital Public Library Of America (FCB Chicago).jpg

extracted.tagline: Unbanning every book in America in real time and putting them back into the hands of the communities that need them most.
extracted.context: Intellectual freedom is under attack in America. More books than ever are being banned off library shelves. Over the past year, thousands of libraries have been forced to ban titles […].
extracted.insight: Using PEN America's Index of Book Bans, the Digital Public Library of America made the most banned books in the U.S. then geofenced the exact footprint of every library with banned books, so that any person who walks into those libraries gains instant access to the banned book.
extracted.idea: The Banned Book Club is designed for today and for the future. Whenever a new book is banned, it can be added to our dynamic database, making it available instantly in any library that censors it, even nationwide. As the campaign grows, access is expanding to all of America, regardless of location.
execution: Geofenced library buildings nationwide
Mobile-app entry point with a QR code at every banned library
Dynamic database that adds newly banned titles in real time
Launched with President Obama and surfaced bestseller titles such as 'Revival' by Stephen King

inferred.one_liner: Walk into a library, unban the banned book.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Used geofencing to circumvent local book bans inside the very libraries enforcing them; reached 1.5B local impressions and unbanned more than 3,000 titles, with a Presidential endorsement amplifying coverage.

visual.style_description: Hand holding a smartphone displaying a redacted Cormac McCarthy-style green-and-white book cover at centre, set against a dark grid background of redacted book pages; left column carries Problem/Idea/Concept text blocks, right column stacks the five large metric figures, and a bottom photo strip shows library activations and supporter logos.

idea.rationale: Geofencing the exact footprint of every library that bans a book so the banned title becomes instantly available the moment a reader walks in is a precise legal-and-spatial loophole built into the user journey. The idea earns its strength from specificity — the reach radius is the floor plan of the censoring library itself — rather than from a generic 'access' message.

strategy.rationale: The Digital Public Library of America is a non-profit digital library coalition whose stated mission is to make America's cultural heritage freely accessible online, so circumventing local book bans inside the very buildings enforcing them is the literal expression of that mission rather than borrowed advocacy. The board anchors the case on PEN America's Index of Book Bans as the canonical source, which gives the data spine of the system a verifiable, third-party foundation.

execution.rationale: The technical layer is a geofencing system over 2,314 libraries tied to a dynamic database that auto-publishes newly banned titles, surfaced through QR codes at participating library entrances and a mobile-app entry point. As Digital Craft the build is functional but the board does not showcase distinctive interaction craft — no native typography, motion design, custom interface idiom or shader-level treatment is named; the win is the system architecture rather than the digital artistry of the product surface.

impact.rationale: The headline outcomes are correctly business-side for an access campaign: 3,059 books unbanned, 997 of them on racial issues, across 674 banned libraries — these are real behaviour-change counts rather than surrogates. The 1.5B local media impressions are entrant-reported and unverified in the Pattern-2 sense, and the Obama-endorsement amplification reads as a press lift rather than as a measurable adoption metric, so the impact case carries on the verifiable unbanning counts more than on the broadcast figures.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the geofence-the-library loophole is a clean activist mechanism with verifiable PEN-sourced unbanning counts, but the digital-craft layer is more system than surface.

one_liner: A free style guide for authentic Black hair in games.
search_doc:
A free style guide for authentic Black hair in games.
Mechanisms: open-source-design-system, representation-as-craft-asset, industry-tooling-as-advocacy, purpose-driven-product, cultural-reference-library
Idea: Let Black gamers see their beauty accurately realised in video games by helping Black creators set the standard for Black hair.
Insight: Black hair in games has been a stereotyped afterthought because game studios lack the reference assets to render it accurately — give them a free, properly-built reference library and the industry standard moves with it.

extracted.tagline: The world's first free, step-by-step guide to the creation and celebration of Black Hair within video games.
extracted.context: Millions of Black people play video games. Despite Nia, the depiction of Black hair in gaming is often still inaccurate and highly stereotypical. In fact, 85% of Black Gamers now feel Black hair is poorly represented in video games.
extracted.idea: Let Black gamers see their beauty accurately realised in video games by helping Black creators set the standard for Black hair.
execution: Created in partnership with Open Source Afro Hair Library and Black 3D artists
Step-by-step guide to authentic Black hair creation in video games
360-page guide
360-degree reference photography
Step-by-step tutorials
Working files, all free for developers to download and use
Industry tools rolled out to better represent the millions of Black gamers
metrics: 85% of Black gamers now feel better represented
95% of Black gamers see the positive societal impact

inferred.insight: Black hair in games has been a stereotyped afterthought because game studios lack the reference assets to render it accurately — give them a free, properly-built reference library and the industry standard moves with it.
inferred.one_liner: A free style guide for authentic Black hair in games.
inferred.qualitative_summary: A 360-page open-source reference guide built by Black creators that gave game studios the assets to render Black hair accurately; drove 1.81B+ impressions and a +32% loyalty lift while shifting an industry-wide craft standard.

visual.style_description: Twin photographic portraits of two Black women with naturally styled hair flanked by a row of in-game character renders that step the look into video-game style; clean off-white background, press logo strip at the top, three labelled text blocks on the left and a metrics column on the right.

idea.rationale: Open-sourcing the missing reference library that game studios need to model Black hair accurately treats a representation problem as a tooling problem — fixable upstream of any single title. The conceit is durable rather than spectacle-led; the value is that any studio can use the asset forever, which is a textbook purpose-driven-product idea applied with a specific craft target.

strategy.rationale: Dove is Unilever's global personal-care brand whose 'Real Beauty' platform has anchored advocacy on hair representation for years (the Crown Coalition, the CROWN Act), so a free industry guide for Black hair in video games is a precise extension of that mission into a category where Dove does not sell product. The case is grounded in a cited proprietary statistic — 85% of Black gamers feel Black hair is poorly represented — which gives the strategic insight a quantified anchor.

execution.rationale: The artefact is a 360-page guide bundled with 360-degree reference photography, step-by-step tutorials and working files for game developers to download free, co-created with the Open Source Afro Hair Library and Black 3D artists. Craft sits in the rigour of the reference assets and in the editorial system of the guide, but as Digital Craft the entry leans on document and PDF craft rather than on a distinctive digital interface, motion language or interactive system — the work is closer to design-system publishing than to interactive craft.

impact.rationale: The board mixes business-side and surrogate metrics: 1.81B earned impressions is entrant-reported and unverified (Pattern 2), the 85% / 95% sentiment figures are recycled survey points lacking a disclosed cohort size (Pattern 1), but the +32% brand loyalty among Black consumers points at a real Dove business outcome and is the load-bearing number. The press footprint across Rolling Stone, Vogue Business, Forbes and gamesindustry.biz is a credible signal that the industry-tooling target was reached.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the open-source guide is exact Dove-mission-fit on a verified gamer insight, but as a digital-craft entry the artefact is publication-craft rather than interactive-craft.

one_liner: The film's website IS a scrollable novel.
search_doc:
The film's website IS a scrollable novel.
Mechanisms: scroll-as-narrative, website-as-literary-object, kinetic-typography, sound-reactive-text, film-extension-as-craft
Idea: This website aimed to create a new form of expression that cannot be achieved through the medium of film. After thorough examination of how the film came to life, what it aimed to portray, and of the film's cultural values, it became clear that the best way of expressing all of these components on a website platform was to create a "scroll book." A novel featuring 353 days of Hirayama's days — apart from the 12 illustrated in the film — were written specifically for the website, allowing viewers to enjoy the contents regardless of whether they have seen the film or not.
Insight: A film's official site is usually a marketing afterthought; treating it as a parallel literary object — a novel written for scrolling — extends the film's quiet, contemplative pace into a medium that normally fights it.

full_record.file_path: 2024/DIGITAL CRAFT/BRONZE/Days Of Hirayama -  Perfect Days (Dentsu Tokyo).jpg

extracted.tagline: A scrollable book created by reassembling a movie's sense of time and storyline
extracted.context: The official website for Wim Wenders' most recent film PERFECT DAYS — gaining worldwide attention after receiving the Best Actor Award at the 76th Cannes International Film Festival and nominated for Best International Feature at the 96th Academy Awards — takes a different approach in expressing what the film tries to portray. It explores the various forms of expression that can only be realised on a digital platform.
extracted.idea: This website aimed to create a new form of expression that cannot be achieved through the medium of film. After thorough examination of how the film came to life, what it aimed to portray, and of the film's cultural values, it became clear that the best way of expressing all of these components on a website platform was to create a "scroll book." A novel featuring 353 days of Hirayama's days — apart from the 12 illustrated in the film — were written specifically for the website, allowing viewers to enjoy the contents regardless of whether they have seen the film or not.
execution: Scroll-driven "scroll book" website rendering 353 days of Hirayama's life
Page reacts to touch or cursor movement on smartphone or laptop
Words sway and create sounds as the viewer scrolls, immersing them in another world
Designed to stimulate sight, sound, and senses simultaneously
metrics: JPY 750 million box office in Japan (as of end of January 2024)
press_coverage: @hktchr_ ("The interactivity drew me into the world of the movie even more than the movie did. I was so blown away that I don't have the words to describe it.")
@takahirolling ("I read all the way to the end, captivated by the prose, sound, and design. I saw myself in the story's protagonist, which heightened my interest in the film.")
@camimu ("Through words and sound alone, you can vividly picture the way the protagonist moves and lives.")

inferred.insight: A film's official site is usually a marketing afterthought; treating it as a parallel literary object — a novel written for scrolling — extends the film's quiet, contemplative pace into a medium that normally fights it.
inferred.one_liner: The film's website IS a scrollable novel.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reframes a film's promo site as a parallel literary work; 353 days of bespoke prose drew 810k views and 280k users in one month and travelled alongside the film through its Cannes and Oscar campaigns.

visual.style_description: Dark, cinematic full-bleed board where scrolling Japanese characters spiral into the page like falling rain, evoking the website's kinetic-text concept directly; serif white title "Days of HIRAYAMA" sits top-left, body-copy columns (Background, Idea, Strategy, Execution, Result) anchor the lower half, with a thumbnail strip of website states at the bottom and short viewer quotes pinned to the right.

idea.rationale: Treating a film's official website as a parallel literary work — a scroll-driven novel of 353 days written specifically for the medium — is an unusually patient reframe of what a promo site can be. The conceit matches the film's contemplative, time-attentive register (Wim Wenders' protagonist Hirayama lives a quiet repetitive life), so the scroll-as-time metaphor is internally coherent rather than imposed.

strategy.rationale: PERFECT DAYS is a Wim Wenders film distributed by Bitters End / Master Mind that won Best Actor at Cannes 2023 and was nominated for the 96th Academy Awards Best International Feature, and its marketing platform sells the film's meditative slowness as its core proposition. The site is mission-coherent with that platform, but the board does not surface an evidenced audience insight beyond the assertion that a website can express things a film cannot — the strategic case is intuited rather than data-grounded.

execution.rationale: The build is a scroll-driven 'scroll book' that renders 353 newly written days of bespoke prose, with kinetic typography that sways and emits sound in response to touch or cursor movement, designed to engage sight and hearing simultaneously on smartphone and laptop. As Digital Craft the work is native — scroll velocity is the reading rhythm and the type is the cinematography — though the board does not detail underlying technique (the easing curves, audio synthesis, or rendering stack), so the craft is asserted more than dissected.

impact.rationale: 810,000 views and 280,000 users in one month are real audience-side figures rather than impression counts, and the JPY 750M Japanese box office is a credible business proxy though it is the film's outcome rather than the website's, so attribution is loose (Pattern 3). The press field is three audience testimonials with no top-tier outlet coverage, which limits the verifiable cultural-reach claim relative to typical Cannes Bronze impact cases.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the scroll-as-novel reframe is genuinely native to the medium and on-brief with the film's quiet register, even as the impact case mixes the website's traffic with the film's box office.

one_liner: A concept car where the car itself is the safety feature.
search_doc:
A concept car where the car itself is the safety feature.
Mechanisms: product-as-protest, concept-car-as-message, ar-as-evidence, auto-show-hijack
Idea: To confront the present problem, we created the car to highlight the double standards Black people experience, during the most common interaction with police - traffic stops.
Insight: The same vehicle features that protect white drivers can endanger Black drivers during traffic stops; the car itself must be redesigned around the disparity in how the encounter unfolds.

full_record.file_path: 2024/DIGITAL CRAFT/SILVER/Driving While Black - Courageous  Conversarion Global Foundation (Goodby Silverstein And Partners, San Francisco _ Critical Mass, Calgary).jpg

extracted.tagline: The first vehicle of its kind where the safety feature is the car itself.
extracted.context: In America, Black drivers are 3x more likely to be killed during an encounter with police. Since 2017, more than 800 Black Americans have been murdered during routine traffic stops.
extracted.idea: To confront the present problem, we created the car to highlight the double standards Black people experience, during the most common interaction with police - traffic stops.
execution: Concept car prototype designed with safety features removed (no steering wheel, no glove box, removed cruise, transparent doors)
Launched at the Detroit Auto Show
DWB billboards and OOH placements wrapping the streets of Detroit
QR codes leading viewers to an AR experience and website
AR viewer letting people sit in the driver's seat and see infamous real-life events

inferred.insight: The same vehicle features that protect white drivers can endanger Black drivers during traffic stops; the car itself must be redesigned around the disparity in how the encounter unfolds.
inferred.one_liner: A concept car where the car itself is the safety feature.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Hijacked the Detroit Auto Show with a concept car stripped of features that endanger Black drivers, anchored by an AR experience that placed viewers behind the wheel of real-life traffic-stop incidents.

visual.style_description: Dark cinematic hero of a sedan-shaped concept car lit on a black stage, surrounded by four product-detail callouts (steering wheel, removed glove box, removed cruise, transparent doors); two short text columns titled 'The Issue' and 'The Idea' beneath, with a bottom strip of OOH, AR-app and Auto-Show photography.

idea.rationale: Designing a concept car whose 'safety feature' is the deliberate removal of features that endanger Black drivers during traffic stops is a sharp reframe of a vehicle as a piece of evidence. The conceit takes the iconography of an auto-show reveal — a polished prototype on a stage — and inverts the genre so that subtractions, not additions, do the storytelling.

strategy.rationale: The Courageous Conversation Global Foundation is a US racial-equity organisation whose mission is to surface structural racism in everyday institutions, so building an artefact around the most common Black-police interaction — the routine traffic stop — is exact mission-fit. The insight rests on a hard, verifiable statistic cited on the board (Black drivers 3x more likely to be killed during police encounters; 800+ killed during routine traffic stops since 2017), and the choice to argue through the car rather than through victims keeps the proposition specific to a policy lever the audience can hold.

execution.rationale: The build ladders a physical prototype (no steering wheel, no glove box, removed cruise, transparent doors) into an auto-show reveal at Detroit, OOH wrapping the city, QR-anchored AR that places the viewer in the driver's seat of real traffic-stop incidents, and a campaign website. Craft is solid across artefact, environment and AR layer, but the digital craft case itself — what makes the AR experience native to its medium rather than a film-in-AR — is sketched rather than evidenced on the board.

impact.rationale: The board's metrics field is empty: no reach, no policy outcome, no audience-engagement figures are disclosed, and the only press cited are Adweek and Creapils. For a campaign whose business outcome is cultural pressure on policing policy, the absence of any quantification — petitions signed, AR sessions completed, legislative coverage — leaves the impact case resting almost entirely on the artefact and the auto-show stage.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the concept-car-as-protest is a precise piece of activist craft on a verified statistic, but the impact case is unevidenced on the board.

one_liner: A signing avatar becomes Japan's emergency alert system.
search_doc:
A signing avatar becomes Japan's emergency alert system.
Mechanisms: accessibility-first-as-craft, real-time-avatar-translation, mocap-as-language-engine, public-broadcast-utility, inclusive-design-as-product
Idea: In order to provide fundamental and equal access to Japan's 300,000+ Deaf Community Members, NHK (Japan's Public Broadcaster) created KIKI. The world's first, text-to-sign translator for Emergency Alerts. Through a first-of-its-kind translation engine, KIKI, a lifelike virtual avatar, receives text input and generates full dynamic Japanese Sign Language in real time. JSL that takes into account the language's unique syntax, importance of pausing and evocation of emotion. Built on a foundation of 16,000 individually captured mocaps of signed words, KIKI is capable of generating disaster messaging within less than 10 seconds.
Insight: Closed captioning fails Deaf communities because reading written Japanese is, for native signers, a translation into a foreign language — true equal access requires native sign delivered as fast as the alert itself.

full_record.file_path: 2024/DIGITAL CRAFT/SILVER/Kiki, Virtual Human Interpreter - NHK Enterprises (Mediamonks, Hilversum).jpg

extracted.tagline: The world's first, text-to-sign translator for Emergency Alerts.
extracted.context: In the last decade Japan has experienced over 30 earthquakes, including a record-breaking one in 2011. As recently as January 1, 2024, the country experienced a 7.6 magnitude quake causing landslides and widespread devastation. Despite advanced early-warning systems designed to provide life-saving information in multiple languages, one very important one was missing: Japanese Sign Language. For the Deaf, providing only closed captioning during Emergency Alerts is like translating content into an entirely different language, as they read. Transcribing spoken words simply doesn't capture the same meaning or nuances for those who communicate primarily through Sign Languages.
extracted.idea: In order to provide fundamental and equal access to Japan's 300,000+ Deaf Community Members, NHK (Japan's Public Broadcaster) created KIKI. The world's first, text-to-sign translator for Emergency Alerts. Through a first-of-its-kind translation engine, KIKI, a lifelike virtual avatar, receives text input and generates full dynamic Japanese Sign Language in real time. JSL that takes into account the language's unique syntax, importance of pausing and evocation of emotion. Built on a foundation of 16,000 individually captured mocaps of signed words, KIKI is capable of generating disaster messaging within less than 10 seconds.
execution: Real-time text-to-JSL translation engine
Lifelike virtual avatar driven by 16,000 mocap-captured signed words
Deployed on January 1, 2024 earthquake Emergency Alert
Direct messaging integration
KIKI safety videos on board aircraft
KIKI guiding Tokyo Museums
Tokyo Deaflympics 2025 ambassador role
metrics: Supports over 300,000+ Deaf individuals in Japan

inferred.insight: Closed captioning fails Deaf communities because reading written Japanese is, for native signers, a translation into a foreign language — true equal access requires native sign delivered as fast as the alert itself.
inferred.one_liner: A signing avatar becomes Japan's emergency alert system.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Replaced text-only emergency alerts with a real-time JSL avatar trained on 16,000 mocapped signs, deployed during a live 7.6-magnitude earthquake and extended into museum guidance, in-flight safety and a Tokyo Deaflympics ambassador role.

visual.style_description: Hero CGI portrait of KIKI, a Japanese woman avatar in a brown vest mid-sign, set against a cool blue gradient with the giant 'KIKI' logo above and a stylized epicenter map of Japan; problem/solution/impact text columns sit on either side and a documentary photo strip of real deployments runs along the bottom.

idea.rationale: Building a real-time text-to-Japanese-Sign-Language avatar so a Deaf audience receives emergency alerts in their first language is a clean, on-brief instance of accessibility-first-as-craft. The idea is functional rather than spectacle-led, which is exactly right for the use case — the win lies in matching the medium to a specific underserved community rather than in any rhetorical twist.

strategy.rationale: NHK Enterprises is the commercial arm of Japan's national public broadcaster, whose public-service mandate explicitly includes equal-access information delivery, so a sign-language system for emergency alerts is core mission, not adjacent purpose. The board grounds the case in verifiable context — over 30 earthquakes in a decade, the 7.6-magnitude New Year's Day 2024 quake, 300,000+ Deaf community members in Japan — and articulates a credible linguistic insight that captioning is itself a translation into a second language for native JSL signers.

execution.rationale: The translation engine is built on 16,000 individually mocap-captured signed words and renders full dynamic JSL — with syntax, pauses and emotional inflection — in under 10 seconds per alert, well inside the latency window the use case demands. The deployment ladders from a live January 1, 2024 earthquake alert through direct-messaging integration into adjacent public-service surfaces (in-flight safety videos, Tokyo museum guidance, Tokyo Deaflympics 2025 ambassador role), turning a single avatar into a portable accessibility utility.

impact.rationale: The quantified outcomes on the board are craft-spec (16,000 mocapped words, <2-second motion phase rendering, 10-second total generation) rather than community-side, so reach into and adoption by the 300,000+ Deaf audience is asserted by addressable population, not by measured usage. There is no disclosed figure for alerts served, Deaf viewers reached during the January 1 deployment, or user satisfaction within the JSL community, leaving the business-outcome side of accessibility lighter than the engineering side.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the 16,000-mocap JSL avatar is a credible accessibility-as-craft engineering feat deployed in a live disaster, even as adoption-side metrics for the Deaf audience are not quantified.

one_liner: An F1 driver races the AI ghosts of racing legends.
search_doc:
An F1 driver races the AI ghosts of racing legends.
Mechanisms: ai-powered-resurrection, data-as-content, real-time-telemetry-cgi, broadcast-as-product, generative-sports-event
Idea: Digitize decades of racing history: a 70-lap sprint race at the Vivint Smart Home Arena raced against AI bots produced from real F1 telemetry — Mario Andretti, Roger Penske, Alain Prost, Senna, Lauda, Schumacher, Hamilton — engineered to react to Logan's every move so each AI matches the era it represents and historic moves resurface lap by lap; race ends with a championship trophy.
Insight: Long-time racing fans crave a generational duel that pits modern champions against the legends they were built on — only AI and telemetry data can finally stage it.

full_record.file_path: 2024/DIGITAL CRAFT/GOLD/Lap Of Legend - Ab Inbev - Michelob Ultra (FCB New York).jpg

extracted.tagline: A first-of-its-kind race that reinvented the world's fastest sport.
extracted.context: An F1 driver faces the legends who created him in a race created with next-generation AR and analytics of 40 years of racing.
extracted.idea: Digitize decades of racing history: a 70-lap sprint race at the Vivint Smart Home Arena raced against AI bots produced from real F1 telemetry — Mario Andretti, Roger Penske, Alain Prost, Senna, Lauda, Schumacher, Hamilton — engineered to react to Logan's every move so each AI matches the era it represents and historic moves resurface lap by lap; race ends with a championship trophy.
execution: Race broadcast live and streamed across networks
AI-powered racing helmet integration
Most intelligent AI racers to date
True-to-life race physics and vehicle design
Six cars, six unique sonic identities
Multi-constellation race-grade GPS
Creating an everlasting Capi-AI strategy
Defying the rules of F1 entertainment
metrics: 1 huge moment that made television history

inferred.insight: Long-time racing fans crave a generational duel that pits modern champions against the legends they were built on — only AI and telemetry data can finally stage it.
inferred.one_liner: An F1 driver races the AI ghosts of racing legends.
inferred.qualitative_summary: An AI-rendered head-to-head race between a current F1 driver and historical legends turned proprietary telemetry data into a televised sports spectacle, drawing 19M+ viewers and 4.5B+ global impressions for a beer brand.

visual.style_description: Cinematic dark hero image of a futuristic open-wheel race car spotlit on a stage with the 'Lap of Legends' chrome title arched above; press logo strip across the top, dense metric column on the right, and a wide thumbnail strip at the bottom showcasing each tech pillar of the race.

idea.rationale: Pitting a current F1 driver against AI reconstructions of seven era-defining legends — Andretti, Penske, Prost, Senna, Lauda, Schumacher, Hamilton — staged on a single track is a vivid use of telemetry as a generational-duel device. The conceit is less category-redefining than tightly executed; AI-resurrection has been done elsewhere, but the choice to make each AI rival behave as the era it represents gives the idea genuine narrative spine.

strategy.rationale: Michelob Ultra is AB InBev's premium light-lager brand whose long-standing platform fuses beer with endurance and elite sport (Super Bowl, Wimbledon, the McLaren F1 partnership), so building a televised motorsport spectacle around its driver Logan is on-brand sports-marketing rather than borrowed purpose. The strategic case is weaker on insight veracity: the board does not cite data behind the 'fans crave a generational duel' premise, leaning on intuition about racing fandom rather than evidence. Mission-fit is solid; insight rigour is thin.

execution.rationale: The technical stack is unusually deep for an entertainment activation: 42.3 million telemetry data points reconstructing 40 years of racing into six era-specific AI drivers, true-to-life vehicle physics, multi-constellation race-grade GPS, an AI-powered racing helmet, and six bespoke sonic identities differentiating the cars on broadcast. Orchestration ladders the artefact through a live televised race, simulcast streams and a press loop across ESPN, Variety, Forbes, WSJ and Sports Illustrated, so the craft achievement is delivered into the format motorsport fans actually consume.

impact.rationale: The headline claims — 19M+ TV viewers, 4.5B+ global impressions, $30M return on media investment — are entrant-reported and unaudited in the classic Pattern-2 sense, but the breadth of unpaid coverage across ESPN, WSJ and Sports Illustrated is a credible signal of cultural reach. The $30M ROI figure points at a real business outcome (efficiency of media spend) rather than only surrogate reach, though the base for that calculation is not disclosed on the board. Impressions inflate the case; the ROI line anchors it.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because 42M telemetry points and era-specific AI rivals translate a beer-brand sports activation into a genuine live-broadcast spectacle, even as the insight is intuited rather than evidenced.

one_liner: An identity that mutates like the octopus it draws.
search_doc:
An identity that mutates like the octopus it draws.
Mechanisms: generative-identity-system, mascot-as-system, variable-typography, motion-as-brand
Idea: MullenLowe's new identity emphasises the fluid and ever-changing nature of the octopus motif, the agency's longstanding mascot. The identity rebuild treats the octopus as a system: an ability to shift its form at will, an ability to adapt to its environment — and our agency, in turn, adapts.
Insight: An agency's identity should behave like the work it sells — adapt, mutate and recombine — so the octopus mark becomes a generative system rather than a fixed logo.

extracted.tagline: MullenLowe's ever-changing visual identity: an ode to the octopus' fluidity and adaptability.
extracted.context: Our objective was to move away from the corporate motifs and develop a new, more visceral identity that would compel to remain globally yet endure enough to be embraced by every region within the network.
extracted.idea: MullenLowe's new identity emphasises the fluid and ever-changing nature of the octopus motif, the agency's longstanding mascot. The identity rebuild treats the octopus as a system: an ability to shift its form at will, an ability to adapt to its environment — and our agency, in turn, adapts.
execution: New octopus-based wordmark and ever-changing visual identity system
Custom animated typeface (The Type)
Mobile app and digital touchpoints (The Apps)
Printed and physical surfaces / paving (The Space)
Murals and large-scale brand environments (The Wall)
Branded apparel and merchandise (The Wear)
press_coverage: The most talked about agency rebrand in 2023 (Webrunner)

inferred.insight: An agency's identity should behave like the work it sells — adapt, mutate and recombine — so the octopus mark becomes a generative system rather than a fixed logo.
inferred.one_liner: An identity that mutates like the octopus it draws.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Replaced a static agency logo with a generative octopus-based system spanning custom type, motion and physical surfaces; delivered 1B earned impressions, 96.3% positive sentiment and a 35% lift in new-business opportunities.

visual.style_description: Black-and-white editorial layout dominated by hand-drawn looping octopus-tentacle marks; an oversized expressive 'MullenLowe' wordmark anchors the bottom while a 3x3 grid of identity variations runs along the right edge; lower strip shows app screens, paving, typography specimens and apparel applications.

idea.rationale: Reimagining the agency's long-standing octopus mascot as a generative, ever-changing identity system rather than a fixed logo is a credible 'mascot-as-system' move, but the broader 'mutating brand identity' idea is well-trodden territory in agency self-rebrands. Originality sits in the choice of octopus-as-organism over the more common abstract-grid generative system, not in the conceit itself.

strategy.rationale: MullenLowe is the IPG-owned global agency network whose long-running market positioning leans on the 'hyperbundled' creative challenger story, and the octopus has been its mascot for years, so an octopus-led rebuild is mission-continuous rather than reset. The strategic case is the lightest axis because the brief is an agency rebranding itself — the audience is hypothetical clients rather than a third-party user — and the board does not cite client research or category data behind the move, so the insight is internal rather than evidenced.

execution.rationale: The system spans a custom animated typeface, a mobile app, large-scale murals, paving and apparel, all expressed through hand-drawn looping tentacle marks and a generative wordmark that adapts to each surface. The craft case is coherent across digital, print and environment, but the board does not detail what makes the digital expression itself distinctive — the rules of the generative system, the motion language, the type's variable axes — so as a Digital Craft entry the artefact is shown more than the technique behind it.

impact.rationale: The reported outcomes mix entrant-reported reach (1B earned impressions, 2,530 social mentions) with surrogate-sentiment claims (96.3% positive sentiment, 300% Google brand surge) where the base is not anchored on the board (Pattern 1). The strongest impact line is +35% in new-business opportunities, which is the right business outcome for an agency rebrand, though the absolute number behind that percentage is not disclosed. Webrunner's 'most talked about agency rebrand of 2023' is positioned as a press accolade, not an audited ranking.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the octopus-as-system rebuild is a coherent multi-surface identity exercise, even as the digital-craft technique behind the generative system is asserted more than shown.

one_liner: The first music video coded inside an Excel spreadsheet.
search_doc:
The first music video coded inside an Excel spreadsheet.
Mechanisms: software-as-canvas, platform-jailbreak, b2b-direct-targeting, code-as-craft, media-as-product
Idea: Spreadbeats: a never-been-done-before, eye- and ear-opening way to prove the power of combining video and audio in a medium perfectly tailored to its audience. We partnered with Coachella headliner and former video game composer Sam Spiegel to score a four-minute music video set entirely inside an Excel spreadsheet, turning rows and columns into a canvas that sprang to life when the file was opened.
Insight: Media planners live in spreadsheets. Every year, the Spotify Advertising storefront sends thousands of spreadsheets with media plan proposals to potential clients. So, to win over media planners, we brought Spotify's video ad capabilities and its ROI superiority to the place where they spend the most time considering ad buys: their trusty old spreadsheets.

extracted.tagline: The first music video entirely coded and experienced in Excel.
extracted.context: In 2023, Spotify launched new video ad units, but there is a problem: media planners and advertisers consider us as just an audio platform. Avid audio-only commitments yield diminished returns. As a result, when reaching out for proposals, they often request media plans tailored exclusively for audio ads. That's a shame because audio ads combined with video on Spotify are 2.2 times more effective than audio ads alone.
extracted.insight: Media planners live in spreadsheets. Every year, the Spotify Advertising storefront sends thousands of spreadsheets with media plan proposals to potential clients. So, to win over media planners, we brought Spotify's video ad capabilities and its ROI superiority to the place where they spend the most time considering ad buys: their trusty old spreadsheets.
extracted.idea: Spreadbeats: a never-been-done-before, eye- and ear-opening way to prove the power of combining video and audio in a medium perfectly tailored to its audience. We partnered with Coachella headliner and former video game composer Sam Spiegel to score a four-minute music video set entirely inside an Excel spreadsheet, turning rows and columns into a canvas that sprang to life when the file was opened.
execution: Original music video composed and rendered inside Microsoft Excel cells
VBA scripting drives cell-by-cell visual choreography
Distributed directly to media planners and ad-buyers as an .xlsx file
Companion site to schedule a meeting with Spotify Ad Sales
Outreach via emails and LinkedIn to ad buyers and recipients
metrics: 1 million data pixels turned into ads
All within one Excel file of 10MB

inferred.one_liner: The first music video coded inside an Excel spreadsheet.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned a four-minute music video into a self-contained Excel file delivered straight to media planners, hijacking the medium they use most to make ad-buy decisions and proving Spotify's video ad capabilities in their own workflow.

visual.style_description: Hero shot of a laptop displaying a music video rendered as a pink-and-green pixel mosaic inside an open Excel spreadsheet, set against a bright lime green crt-grid background; pixel-font title 'SPREADBEATS' anchors the top, with three left-column blocks (Challenge, Context, Idea, Origins) of dense small body copy, and a bottom strip of six execution thumbnails.

idea.rationale: Composing and rendering a four-minute music video natively inside a Microsoft Excel spreadsheet is a category-defining reframe of where craft can live — the medium of the ad-buy decision is jailbroken into the canvas. The reveal lands instantly because the audience opens an .xlsx and is hit with a coordinated cell-by-cell musical performance, an outcome no off-the-shelf tool was built to produce.

strategy.rationale: Spotify is the global audio-streaming brand whose 2023 push into video ad units is the explicit business problem behind the work; the idea is exact mission-fit because it argues for Spotify's video offering using Spotify's own combined-format ROI argument (audio plus video is 2.2x more effective than audio alone). The insight that media planners live in spreadsheets is a plausible B2B observation rather than a data-cited fact, which is the soft spot — it is treated as self-evident on the board rather than sourced.

execution.rationale: The build is a single 10MB .xlsx file with roughly one million data-pixel cells driven by VBA scripting, choreographed to an original Sam Spiegel score and engineered to play without external dependencies on a media planner's existing machine. The orchestration is just as native: the file is direct-distributed by email and LinkedIn to the exact ad-buyer cohort the campaign needs to convert, with a companion site closing the loop to a Spotify Ad Sales meeting — every layer of the campaign lives where the target already works.

impact.rationale: The board's quantified outcomes are craft-side rather than business-side — '1 million data pixels turned into ads' and '10MB' describe the artefact, not adoption or revenue. There is no disclosed figure for meetings booked, planner conversion, or revenue from the ad buyers targeted, so the proven business result a B2B sales campaign is built around is conspicuously absent. The craft, novelty and earned trade-press resonance carry the case, but the impact section is the lightest part of an otherwise GP-grade record.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins the Grand Prix because it turns Microsoft Excel itself into a working Spotify ad unit, delivered into the exact file format media planners use to decide ad buys.

brand: ANP / WWF & Jardim Zoológico de Lisboa
one_liner: Each letter fades as its animal nears extinction.
search_doc:
Each letter fades as its animal nears extinction.
Mechanisms: data-driven-typography, type-as-interface, endangered-species-as-glyph, generative-conservation-tool, media-as-product
Idea: A typographic font directly linked to the database of the IUCN Red List of Threatened Species. Each letter represents an animal. The fewer individuals left, the more the letter disappears. People can download the font, make a donation and use it to spread the word.
Insight: For the common person, this tragedy is hard to grasp without context or sense of proportion.

full_record.file_path: 2024/DIGITAL CRAFT/BRONZE/The Endangered Tipeface -Anf _ WWF & Jardim Zoologico De Lisboa (Bar Ogilvy Lisbon).webp

extracted.brand: ANP / WWF & Jardim Zoológico de Lisboa
extracted.tagline: A living font born to protect all animal life.
extracted.context: Earth is facing the 6th mass extinction. 41,000 species are threatened with extinction, and it's urgent to involve society in the global concern.
extracted.insight: For the common person, this tragedy is hard to grasp without context or sense of proportion.
extracted.idea: A typographic font directly linked to the database of the IUCN Red List of Threatened Species. Each letter represents an animal. The fewer individuals left, the more the letter disappears. People can download the font, make a donation and use it to spread the word.
execution: Living typographic font tied to IUCN Red List database
Each letter disappears in proportion to species population
Free font download with donation flow
Limited edition of 26 numbered screen prints, one for each letter
Posters available to people committed to conservation projects
Leveraging IUCN Red List data to visualise endangered species
press_coverage: "A harsh look at the state of decay in the planet's ailing ecosystem"

inferred.one_liner: Each letter fades as its animal nears extinction.
inferred.qualitative_summary: A downloadable typeface where every letter represents a Red List species and degrades in proportion to its remaining population, drawing 10,000+ font downloads, 3.5M+ impressions and coverage in 17 countries.

visual.style_description: Dark moody landscape backdrop of a misty mountain; the hero is a row of decaying chrome letters — I, O, Z, G, E — each labelled with the endangered species it stands for and its remaining population, with the Grevy Zebra Z featured central. Lower band shows a full living alphabet on the left and a dense context/insight/idea block plus metric strip and merch line-up on the right.

idea.rationale: A typeface where each glyph is a Red List species and degrades in proportion to its remaining population turns biodiversity loss into something you can read disappearing. The conceit is genuinely native to type-as-interface — the medium is the message in a literal, measurable way — and the choice to bind one alphabet to one canonical dataset (IUCN Red List) gives the work a clear, ownable rule.

strategy.rationale: Lisbon Zoo (Jardim Zoológico de Lisboa) is Portugal's national zoological institution and ANP / WWF Portugal is the local arm of the global conservation NGO, so a tool that makes species loss visible is exact mission-fit for both partners. The strategic insight on the board — that mass extinction is hard to grasp without context or proportion — is presented as plausible reasoning rather than backed by audience research, though the underlying biodiversity context is anchored on the IUCN Red List, which gives the data side credibility.

execution.rationale: The build wires a typographic system to the IUCN Red List database so glyph integrity is driven by live species-population data, paired with a free font download + donation flow and a limited edition of 26 numbered screen prints, one per letter. As Digital Craft the data-to-type pipeline is the central craft achievement, but the board does not detail the rendering technique (variable-font axes, generative degradation rule, refresh cadence), so the craft case is asserted more than demonstrated at a technical level.

impact.rationale: 10,000+ font downloads is a real adoption metric for a downloadable artefact, but the headline reach numbers (1.1M+ earned media, 3.5M+ impressions, 1.8M+ reach across 17 countries) are entrant-reported and unverified (Pattern 2), and the press field is anonymous critical quotes rather than named outlets, which limits the credibility of the cultural-reach claim. The board does not disclose donations raised or conservation outcomes — the business outcome a partnership of a zoo and WWF would care about — so the impact case rests on downloads and modest reach.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because binding a typeface to live IUCN species data is a clean type-as-interface idea, even as the technical craft and donation-side outcomes are not fully evidenced.

brand: Change The Ref & March For Our Lives
search_doc:
Dead victims call their representatives.
Mechanisms: ai-voice-resurrection, direct-line-to-power, victim-as-author, legislative-advocacy, phone-call-as-medium
Idea: Using AI technology, we re-created the voices of shooting victims from around the country and used them to send phone calls directly to members of Congress. Joining the U.S. Gould People their calls to their elected representatives with a click of a few clicks and their ZIP code using our website, the website [...] AI voices will be sent again and again until change is made.
Insight: Lawmakers ignore statistics and form letters but cannot ignore a phone call from the actual voice of a child their inaction failed to protect.

full_record.file_path: 2024/DIGITAL CRAFT/BRONZE/The Shotline - Change The Ref & March For Our Lives  (MullenLowe Boston).jpg

extracted.brand: Change The Ref & March For Our Lives
extracted.tagline: Voices lost to gun violence make the call for change
extracted.context: In 2023, guns killed 43,016 Americans, and yet Congress passed zero federal gun laws. We knew we couldn't change the laws without convincing the minds of the lawmakers. One of the most effective methods to convince Congress its weaknesses is to pick up the phone and call. Your representatives, so we moved away from traditional, awareness campaigns and turned to new tech that allowed citizens to demand action from their elected officials directly.
extracted.idea: Using AI technology, we re-created the voices of shooting victims from around the country and used them to send phone calls directly to members of Congress. Joining the U.S. Gould People their calls to their elected representatives with a click of a few clicks and their ZIP code using our website, the website [...] AI voices will be sent again and again until change is made.
execution: AI-recreated voices of gun-violence victims used to phone members of Congress
ZIP-code lookup tool on theshotline.org that routes the call to the user's representatives
Calls re-sent on a loop until legislative action is taken
Featured victim voices including Nathaniel Moran, Ted Cruz, John Cornyn (representatives), with victim audio dubbed in

inferred.insight: Lawmakers ignore statistics and form letters but cannot ignore a phone call from the actual voice of a child their inaction failed to protect.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Generated 125K+ calls to Congress and 3.6B organic impressions, weaponising AI voice synthesis to turn gun-violence victims into a persistent direct-pressure mechanism on US lawmakers.

visual.style_description: Stencilled, ransom-note style "The Shotline" headline over a row of cropped portrait photos of young gun-violence victims at the bottom — putting the human cost literally beneath the call-to-action; centre column shows a website mock with a ZIP-code input "Enter your ZIP to send this call to your representatives", flanked by Issue / Idea / Results body copy and a dense press-logo grid on the right.

idea.rationale: Using AI to resurrect the voices of children killed by gun violence and routing those voices into phone calls to their parents' representatives is a viscerally specific lobbying mechanism — the medium of pressure is the voice that should not exist. The conceit ties the technology of synthetic voice to the political channel that is hardest for elected officials to ignore (the constituent phone call), giving the idea both rhetorical force and a working call-to-action.

strategy.rationale: Change The Ref is the gun-reform non-profit founded by Manuel and Patricia Oliver after the Parkland murder of their son Joaquin, and March For Our Lives is the youth-led movement born from the same shooting, so the brands' mission is exactly to convert grief into legislative pressure. The strategic case is anchored on a hard cited statistic (43,016 Americans killed by guns in 2023, zero federal gun laws passed) and on a verifiable lobbying truth that constituent phone calls disproportionately move members of Congress, giving the insight verifiable footing on both ends.

execution.rationale: The build wires an AI voice-synthesis pipeline trained on each victim's recordings to a ZIP-code lookup on theshotline.org that routes calls to the user's specific representatives, with the system re-dialling on a loop until legislation moves. As Digital Craft the work is mechanically inventive but presentation-light — the board itself reads as a stencilled poster more than a digital craft showcase, and the website mock is plain ZIP-input chrome rather than distinctive interface or motion craft. The native-to-Digital-Craft check is the weakest axis here.

impact.rationale: 125,000+ calls to Congress is the right business outcome for a lobbying campaign — a direct-pressure count, not a surrogate — and is the load-bearing number. The 3.6B organic impressions and the press footprint across WSJ, CNN, NPR, AP, Washington Post and CBS News are credible signals of breadth, though all are entrant-reported in the Pattern-2 sense. The 22% increase in gun reform conversation is an unanchored percentage with no disclosed base or measurement window (Pattern 1).

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because routing AI-resurrected victim voices into constituent calls is a precise lobbying mechanism on a hard statistic, even as the digital-craft surface itself is presented more as a poster than as an interactive artefact.

one_liner: Apps that float in your living room.
search_doc:
Apps that float in your living room.
Mechanisms: spatial-ui-as-craft, gaze-and-gesture-input, glass-material-rendering, ambient-environment-integration, controllerless-interaction
Idea: The goal was an interface that kept people connected to each other and the real world, without clunky controllers. The design should be so intuitive that people could immediately pick up this device, understand how to use it, and control it with natural inputs.
Insight: Spatial computing fails if the device feels like a barrier between the user and the room; the interface succeeds only when input becomes invisible — eyes, hands, voice.

extracted.context: Apple Vision Pro and visionOS unlock new ways to interact with technology and the world through spatial computing. Years in the making, the platform is full of technological breakthroughs made possible by the tight integration between design and engineering teams across hardware and software.
extracted.idea: The goal was an interface that kept people connected to each other and the real world, without clunky controllers. The design should be so intuitive that people could immediately pick up this device, understand how to use it, and control it with natural inputs.
execution: Three-dimensional user interface controlled entirely by a person's eyes, hands, and voice
Intuitive gestures: look to select, tap fingers to confirm, flick the wrist to scroll
Virtual keyboard and dictation input; Siri voice control to open or close apps and play media
Glass-material UI grounded in the real world with real-time lighting and shadows
Floating app icon array (TV, Music, Mindfulness, Settings, Freeform, Safari, Photos, Notes, App Store, Mail, Messages, Keynote, Compatible Apps)

inferred.insight: Spatial computing fails if the device feels like a barrier between the user and the room; the interface succeeds only when input becomes invisible — eyes, hands, voice.
inferred.one_liner: Apps that float in your living room.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Sets the craft benchmark for spatial-computing interfaces by rendering app icons as physically lit glass objects inside the user's actual room, with input system built entirely around eyes, hands, and voice.

visual.style_description: Hero render places translucent, real-time-lit visionOS app icons (Music, Photos, Safari, Mail, Messages, App Store and more) floating in a softly lit living room with armchairs and a fireplace, demonstrating the spatial-UI concept literally; minimal text columns sit at the top — Description, Brief, Solution — over a wide cinematic frame.

idea.rationale: Designing visionOS around eyes, hands and voice as the primary input grammar — no controllers, no surrogate devices — is a coherent interface proposition for spatial computing, but as an 'idea' it is essentially a product design thesis rather than a campaign idea. Originality lives in the rigour of the input model rather than in any rhetorical reframe.

strategy.rationale: Apple is the world's largest consumer-electronics brand whose long-standing platform argues that personal computing succeeds when hardware and software are co-designed end to end, so launching visionOS as the first ground-up spatial OS is a literal extension of that mission. The strategic insight — that spatial computing fails if the device feels like a barrier — is presented as a design principle rather than evidenced with user-research data, which is the soft spot in an otherwise on-brand brief.

execution.rationale: The craft case rests on a 3D UI controlled by gaze-and-pinch, a glass-material rendering system with real-time lighting and shadows that anchors floating icons in the user's actual room, and a coherent gesture vocabulary (look-to-select, tap-to-confirm, wrist-flick-to-scroll) extended by Siri and virtual keyboard. Execution as Digital Craft is the strongest axis because the work itself is the interface: every icon, easing curve and material is part of the artefact being judged.

impact.rationale: The board carries no metrics field — no unit sales, no developer adoption, no benchmarks against prior XR interfaces, no usability studies — and no press coverage is cited. For a Digital Craft entry the work can defend itself on the screen, but the absence of any quantified business or behavioural outcome leaves the impact case resting on category-leadership reputation rather than evidenced effect.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the gaze-pinch-voice grammar and real-time-lit glass UI set the spatial-computing craft benchmark, even as the board offers no quantified outcomes to anchor the impact case.

one_liner: One childhood photo becomes the adult who confronts you.
search_doc:
One childhood photo becomes the adult who confronts you.
Mechanisms: ai-aged-victim-as-messenger, deepfake-as-warning, single-data-point-amplification, purpose-driven-product, social-filter-as-solution
Idea: Using the latest AI technology on a single photograph, we created the virtually aged version of a 9-year-old girl, Ella. In our film, she confronts her parents — and by extension parents worldwide — with the consequences of sharing kids' data online. We also created a solution: a social filter pixelating children's faces to help parents to #ShareWithCare.
Insight: Parents who share their children's photos online treat each post as harmless, but a single image is enough raw material for AI to project the child's adult identity — making the abstract privacy risk visceral.

full_record.file_path: 2024/DIGITAL CRAFT/GOLD/Without Consent - A Message From Ella - Deutsche Telekom (adam&eve, Berlin).jpg

extracted.tagline: An AI-generated wake-up call to protect children's digital privacy, created with one piece of data: the photo of a 9-year-old.
extracted.context: Over 75% of parents share their kids' data on social media, without their consent, unaware of how it could impact their future. The rise of AI and deepfake technology makes this sharing behaviour a pressing danger, so Deutsche Telekom, as Europe's biggest telecom provider, wanted to send a wake-up call.
extracted.idea: Using the latest AI technology on a single photograph, we created the virtually aged version of a 9-year-old girl, Ella. In our film, she confronts her parents — and by extension parents worldwide — with the consequences of sharing kids' data online. We also created a solution: a social filter pixelating children's faces to help parents to #ShareWithCare.
execution: AI-generated future-aged version of a 9-year-old girl (Ella) confronting her parents in a film
Social media filter that pixelates children's faces to enable #ShareWithCare
Adoption as an educational tool by European government and police agencies

inferred.insight: Parents who share their children's photos online treat each post as harmless, but a single image is enough raw material for AI to project the child's adult identity — making the abstract privacy risk visceral.
inferred.one_liner: One childhood photo becomes the adult who confronts you.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Generated 3.7 billion impressions across 70+ countries and was adopted as an educational tool by European government and police agencies, shifting the parental over-sharing conversation toward measurable behaviour change.

visual.style_description: Hero close-up portrait of a 9-year-old girl's face overlaid with a glitched, pixel-distorted AI artefact down the centre, dramatising data-as-identity-leak; left column carries challenge/idea/results body copy on black, right column stacks government-agency logos, large metric numbers, press logos, social testimonials, and a QR code.

idea.rationale: Aging a single 9-year-old's photo with AI to make the future-adult version of the child confront her own parents is a viscerally precise translation of abstract data-privacy risk into a face. The conceit weaponises the exact technology it is warning against — deepfake aging as the messenger — giving the idea internal coherence that an awareness film alone could not achieve.

strategy.rationale: Deutsche Telekom is Europe's largest telecom operator whose long-running 'Magenta Moments' / digital-responsibility platform has positioned the brand as a steward of safe connectivity for families, so a wake-up call on parental over-sharing is exact mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The board anchors the case on a real, citable data point — over 75% of parents share their kids' data on social media — and frames the AI-deepfake escalation as a credible emerging vector, giving the strategic rationale verifiable footing on both ends.

execution.rationale: Craft is two artefacts working in tandem: a hero film built from one photo by an aging-and-performance AI pipeline, and a companion social-media filter that pixelates children's faces as a working #ShareWithCare product the audience can install. The orchestration laddered from launch film into earned press across NBC, GMA, The New York Times and Le Monde, then into institutional adoption by European government and police agencies, turning a single asset into a policy-grade tool.

impact.rationale: The reach numbers — 3.7 billion impressions, 51 million reach, 400+ outlets across 70+ countries — are entrant-reported and unaudited, but the press footprint (GMA, NBC, NYT, Le Monde, ELLE, Mediapart) is a credible earned signal that the wake-up-call objective was met. The strongest impact lever is the adoption-as-an-educational-tool line by European government and police agencies, which is a real behaviour-change outcome rather than a surrogate metric, though the board does not quantify it (how many agencies, what installed base).

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because the AI aging pipeline turns one childhood photo into the adult who confronts the parent, weaponising the exact privacy threat the campaign is warning about.
